mcqs.question: According to Flynn's Taxonomy, what does SIMD stand for?

options.text: Single Instruction, Multiple Data
Synchronous Instruction, Multiple Data
Sequential Instruction, Multiple Devices
Systematic Instruction, Managed Data

mcqs.explanation: Flynn's Taxonomy defines SIMD as Single Instruction, Multiple Data. A single control unit broadcasts one instruction to multiple processing elements, each executing it on its own local data.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a key characteristic of a classical SIMD processor array?

options.text: Each Processing Element (PE) fetches its own instructions independently.
A central Control Processor broadcasts instructions to all PEs for lockstep execution.
PEs are designed to execute different instructions simultaneously.
It requires complex hardware for decentralized instruction scheduling.

mcqs.explanation: The defining feature of a classical SIMD machine is a central control unit that fetches an instruction and broadcasts it to all PEs. All PEs then execute this same instruction simultaneously on their own data (lockstep execution).

mcqs.question: A modern CPU with AVX-512 instructions can perform sixteen 32-bit floating-point additions in a single cycle. This is an example of which type of SIMD implementation?

options.text: Processor Array
Distributed MIMD
Packed SIMD
Multithreaded Core

mcqs.explanation: AVX-512 is Packed SIMD: it uses wide 512-bit vector registers to hold multiple data elements (sixteen 32-bit floats). A single instruction operates on all these elements in parallel within the same CPU core.

mcqs.question: What is a major challenge when implementing conditional branches (if-else) on a pure SIMD architecture?

options.text: Instructions become too long to decode.
PEs cannot access memory during branches.
It breaks the lockstep model, forcing all PEs to execute all paths with masking.
The control processor becomes unable to fetch new instructions.

mcqs.explanation: In lockstep SIMD, all PEs must execute the same instruction. For if-else branches, all PEs execute both paths but results are only stored where the condition is true (masking/predication). This wastes cycles when many PEs are idle.

mcqs.question: How does the SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads) model used in GPUs relate to classical SIMD?

options.text: SIMT is identical to SIMD and the terms are interchangeable.
SIMT is a type of MIMD and is fundamentally different from SIMD.
SIMT provides SIMD-like execution but with more flexibility for individual threads.
SIMT allows each thread to have its own independent instruction stream, unlike SIMD.

mcqs.explanation: SIMT implements the SIMD paradigm with a thread-based abstraction. A warp of threads executes the same instruction on different data in lockstep (SIMD-like), but SIMT provides features like independent instruction pointers and hardware-managed branching for more flexibility.

mcqs.question: Which classical SIMD machine had up to 65,536 processing elements?

options.text: ILLIAC IV
Connection Machine CM-2
Cray-1
IBM Blue Gene

mcqs.explanation: The Connection Machine CM-2 (1987) was a massively parallel SIMD machine with up to 65,536 simple processing elements. ILLIAC IV had 64 PEs. The Cray-1 was a vector processor (pipelined SIMD), not a processor array.

mcqs.question: Which of the following workloads is BEST suited for SIMD execution?

options.text: A web server handling different requests from different clients.
Applying the same brightness filter to every pixel of an image.
A database query with complex nested conditional logic.
A multi-player game server managing different player states.

mcqs.explanation: Applying the same filter to every pixel is a perfect data-parallel task: the same instruction is applied to multiple independent data elements. The other options involve different operations or data-dependent branching, which are poorly suited for SIMD.
